data.name: Nikola Vasilj
data.description: Goalkeeping runs in the Vasilj family: Nikola’s father, Vladimir, was part of Croatia’s squads at the 1998 and 2002 World Cups, while Nikola himself represents Bosnia and Herzegovina, something that, in this part of the world, nobody really finds unusual. Unlike many modern elite keepers, Vasilj was never considered a major prodigy and spent years on a treadmill of loans, reserve football and lower league action before finally reaching the Bundesliga with the cult club St Pauli. As a child he was a huge admirer of Manuel Neuer, and eventually he found himself playing against his idol – though St Pauli's recent relegation might put an end to such occasions. Despite his aggressive style and spectacular reflex saves, teammates describe him as unusually calm and reserved away from football.

data.name: Nihad Mujakić
data.description: Few players in the Bosnia and Herzegovina squad have taken a stranger route than Nihad Mujakic, who has somehow ended up playing for Sarajevo, Hajduk Split and Partizan Belgrade, three clubs whose supporters rarely agree on anything beyond football mattering far too much. Inside the Bosnia squad he is probably understood differently than outside it. The national-team coach, Sergej Barbarez, values him less as a polished defender and more as a player capable of handling uncomfortable moments without overthinking them. Every national team tends to carry at least one footballer the supporters argue about constantly – Mujakic may be that player for this generation.

data.name: Dennis Hadžikadunić
data.description: Hadzikadunic’s career has felt unusually unsettled for a player still in his 20s. Since leaving Malmö for Rostov in 2018, he has spent years moving between countries and clubs almost season after season, passing through Sweden, Russia, Spain and Germany before arriving in Italy, largely because Fifa regulations after the invasion of Ukraine repeatedly forced temporary moves away from Rostov. At one stage he admitted he had grown tired of constantly adapting to new dressing rooms, coaches and cities. Born and raised in Malmö to Bosnian parents, Hadzikadunic represented Sweden at every youth level before eventually choosing to switch allegiance to Bosnia and Herzegovina. Calm on the ball and elegant in possession, he has long been viewed as one of the more technically refined defenders in the national team setup.

data.name: Tarik Muharemović
data.description: Muharemovic speaks five languages and carries himself with a calmness that often makes people forget how young he actually is. Born in Slovenia to Bosnian parents, raised in Austria and developed in Italy, his story already stretches across half of Europe. His rise has been remarkably fast: Juventus scouts initially travelled to Austria to watch another player at Wolfsberger, but ended up signing Muharemovic instead. Since then, the left-footed defender has developed into one of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s most highly rated young players, notably comfortable stepping into midfield and progressing the ball under pressure. In Italy there is already a growing feeling that a much bigger transfer is probably only a matter of time.

data.name: Sead Kolašinac
data.description: Nicknamed the Tank, Kolasinac built his reputation on aggression, loyalty and old-school toughness. Born in Karlsruhe to Bosnian parents who fled the war, he worked as a nightclub bouncer as a teenager while coming through Schalke’s academy. At Schalke he became a cult figure because of his uncompromising style and connection with supporters, before later generating global news after confronting armed thieves during an attempted robbery involving his then Arsenal teammate Mesut Özil in London in 2019. One of only two players in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s squad with previous World Cup experience, Kolasinac is also remembered for the unfortunate own goal he scored after just three minutes against Argentina at the Maracanã in the country’s first-ever match at the tournament in 2014.

data.name: Benjamin Tahirović
data.description: Tahirovic’s rise to elite football happened unusually fast. Born in Sweden to Bosnian parents, he went from the lower divisions of Swedish football to José Mourinho’s Roma in the space of only a few years. Ajax then signed him as one of Europe’s most highly rated young midfielders. But his career has already contained more turbulence than most players experience in a decade. After difficult periods in Amsterdam and Denmark he unexpectedly became part of a public controversy before Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup playoff against Wales, when Sergej Barbarez claimed Brøndby’s Welsh coach, Steve Cooper, had wilfully conspired to rule him out of the game. Barbarez later apologised to Cooper, who had in fact dropped the player for failing to “live up to the values” of the Danish club, but the episode revealed how important he had already become for the national team at 23.

data.name: Amar Dedić
data.description: There are two very different sides to Amar Dedic’s character. Off the pitch he comes across as calm, polite and slightly reserved; on it he plays with constant aggression and intensity, always sprinting, pressing and demanding the ball. Coaches have trusted him from a very young age because of that mentality. Born and raised in Austria to a Bosnian family that left the country during the war years, Dedic has often spoken about the sacrifices his parents made while building a new life abroad. Salzburg brought him into their academy at 13, and his journey from there has taken him through the Champions League and Marseille to Benfica. Despite still being only 23, he already has more than 25 caps and enters this World Cup as one of the leaders of a new generation.

data.name: Armin Gigović
data.description: Gigovic spent most of his youth career in Sweden’s national setup, even captaining their under-21s, before eventually deciding to represent Bosnia and Herzegovina at senior level, despite developing alongside a hugely talented generation of Swedes, including names such as Anthony Elanga, Lucas Bergvall and Roony Bardghji. Born in Lund to Bosnian parents, he built his reputation through intensity, pressing and constant running rather than spectacular football. His most important moment in a Bosnia shirt arrived in Bucharest in March 2025. Bosnia entered the World Cup qualifiers without a victory in their previous seven matches and under enormous pressure after years of disappointment. Gigovic scored the only goal against Romania, securing one of the national team’s most eye-catching away wins and changing the mood around the squad almost overnight.

data.name: Samed Baždar
data.description: Partizan supporters started calling Samed Bazdar “the new Vlahovic” before he had even turned 18, at which point the original Vlahovic was himself only 22. Juventus scouts reportedly followed him as a teenager, while Serbian media quickly built the usual Balkan mythology around another tall, technical striker coming through at Belgrade. Bazdar himself once joked that he was “annoying people at Partizan” with constant extra training sessions, adding: “That’s how Dusan did it too.” After representing Serbia at every youth level and even making one senior appearance, his decision to switch to Bosnia and Herzegovina under Sergej Barbarez and Emir Spahic became one of the region’s biggest national-team stories. Technically gifted and unpredictable, Bazdar belongs to the generation Bosnia hopes can help the national team recover from the inevitable departure of Edin Dzeko, even if his club career has recently lost momentum after the early hype surrounding him – he has been only an occasional starter since joining the Polish side Jagiellonia Bialystok on loan in January.

data.name: Ermedin Demirović
data.description: Before Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup playoff Demirovic jokingly promised he would buy beer for the entire Stuttgart stadium if his side qualified. After they defeated Italy he actually followed through, standing behind the bar and personally serving free beer to supporters in Stuttgart. The gesture was particularly notable because Demirovic himself does not drink alcohol. “I’m a man of my word,” he said afterwards. Born in Germany to Bosnian parents, Demirovic is one of the rare players of his generation who represented Bosnia and Herzegovina at every single youth level before eventually reaching the senior national team. His club career was far less straightforward than many expected: released by Hamburg and pushed through loans in France, Spain and Switzerland before eventually establishing himself as one of the Bundesliga’s most complete forwards with Augsburg and Stuttgart.

data.name: Edin Džeko
data.description: Long before he became the defining figure in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s football history, Dzeko was sold by Zeljeznicar to the Czech side Teplice for a relatively small fee because few people inside Bosnian football truly believed a tall, physically underdeveloped striker from Sarajevo could possibly become one of Europe’s elite forwards. He had grown up playing football between buildings and shelters in Sarajevo during the war, later describing football from that period as “an escape from reality”. What followed became the greatest career Bosnian football has produced. A Bundesliga title with Wolfsburg, Premier League triumphs with Manchester City, captaincy at Roma, Champions League finals with Inter; Dzeko eventually became Bosnia's all-time leading appearance-maker and record goalscorer, and remains one of their key players at the age of 40. Inside Bosnia, though, his importance long ago moved beyond football itself. Younger players in Sergej Barbarez’s squad often speak about him less as a teammate and more as the reference point of an entire generation.

data.name: Mladen Jurkas
data.description: Things have moved quickly for this teenager. This time last year, Jurkas was gaining first-team experience in Bosnia and Herzegovina’s second tier; barely 12 months later, he finds himself on the plane to the World Cup. Jurkas made his senior debut for Borac Banja Luka at just 17 and this season established himself as the club’s first-choice goalkeeper. Borac won their fourth league title while he kept 16 clean sheets in 31 appearances – numbers that initially earned him a place on Bosnia’s reserve list for this summer. But after Osman Hadzikic got injured in a warm-up friendly against North Macedonia, Jurkas received a late call-up, turning what had already been a remarkable year into something even more improbable for the 18-year-old.

data.name: Ivan Bašić
data.description: Basic was still a teenager when people around his first club, Zrinjski Mostar, started describing him as “old-fashioned” in the best possible sense – not because of the way he looked or spoke, but because of the way he understood football. While many young midfielders enjoy rolling out tricks or flashy skills, Basic built his reputation through honing positioning, timing and an unusual sense for controlling rhythm. His career has since taken a route very few Bosnian players imagined for themselves. After winning the Bosnian title with Zrinjski, he moved to the Russian side Orenburg during one of the strangest periods in modern European football, before later continuing in Kazakhstan with Astana. Away from the spotlight of major leagues, he quietly developed into one of the more tactically reliable midfielders of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s new generation.

data.name: Ivan Šunjić
data.description: Even before Sunjic arrived in England in 2019, when Birmingham City paid about £7m to bring him to the Championship, he had the reputation of being one of Dinamo Zagreb’s most important young players, a captain, a Croatia international, a midfielder with youth and Champions League experience. Instead of glamorous Premier League nights, though, most of his next four years were defined by survival fights, managerial chaos and some of the most physically demanding football in Europe. Born in Zenica and raised in Croatia, Sunjic switched his international allegiance from Croatia to Bosnia and Herzegovina in 2024. For many supporters the move felt symbolic, as a player once seen as a key part of Croatia’s future chose instead to play for Bosnia even while the national team was in a period of renewal.

data.name: Amar Memić
data.description: Only a few years ago Memic was playing in the second tier of Bosnian football with Radnik Hadzici, far away from the spotlight or serious national-team conversations. His rise since then has been rapid even by modern football standards: Slovenia, then Czech football, then Viktoria Plzen and European competition, all in the space of four years. Sergej Barbarez’s decision to call him up for the senior national team in 2025 initially surprised many supporters in Bosnia and Herzegovina. But inside the squad, Memic quickly earned respect because of the qualities that define almost his entire game: pace, direct running and relentless energy with and without the ball. Barbarez praised his mentality and willingness to work for the team, while teammates describe him as one of the easier personalities to integrate into the dressing room.

data.name: Amir Hadžiahmetović
data.description: When Amir Hadziahmetovic injured his knee during Hull City’s push for the Championship playoffs under Sergej Jakirovic, fears immediately flared that his World Cup was over. The Bosnia and Herzegovina FA publicly confirmed the injury was serious, surgery was expected and recovery in time for the tournament looked impossible. Which made his name appearing on Sergej Barbarez’s final squad list one of the biggest surprises of the announcement. Barbarez later explained that doctors believed Hadziahmetovic could still recover without surgery before the World Cup, though with significant risk for his long-term future. He chose the tournament anyway. That decision probably explains him better than any tactical description could. Calm and intelligent, if rarely spectacular, Hadziahmetovic has spent most of his career being appreciated more by coaches than by highlight compilations. Since leaving Zeljeznicar for Turkey as a teenager, he quietly developed into one of Bosnia’s most tactically disciplined midfielders.

data.name: Dženis Burnić
data.description: Burnic’s football education took place at Borussia Dortmund, one of Europe’s most demanding academies, where he was considered among the club’s more promising midfield prospects during his teenage years. He won multiple youth titles with Dortmund and made his Champions League debut at just 18 – one of only two first-team appearances for the German giants – but his senior career turned into something far less glamorous than many expected: loans, relegation fights and constant battles for stability shaped the first half of his career. Born in Germany to Bosnian parents, Burnic represented the land of his birth throughout the youth categories before switching to Bosnia and Herzegovina in 2024, the result of many years of consideration: after receiving his first senior call-up he admitted that playing for Bosnia had been something he had thought about “since childhood”.

data.name: Nikola Katić
data.description: During Plymouth Argyle’s famous FA Cup victory over Liverpool in 2025, Katic lost a tooth early in the game, kept playing and then swore live on television afterwards because, as he put it himself: “I fucking love it.” It was probably the perfect summary of the defender Bosnia and Herzegovina supporters had already grown to love. Tall, aggressive and uncompromising, Katic belongs to a generation of centre-backs that treats defending as a personal battle. Before choosing Bosnia at senior level, he represented Croatia in youth categories and built his early career through the lower leagues of Croatian football before Slaven Belupo gave him his breakthrough. Rangers later signed him during Steven Gerrard’s rebuild of the club, where his physical style quickly made him an Ibrox favourite.

data.name: Kerim Alajbegović
data.description: Before most supporters in Bosnia and Herzegovina had even seen him play a full senior match, clips of Alajbegovic’s performances had already spread across social media. The hype only grew after Bayer Leverkusen, having honed him since 2021, sold him to Red Bull Salzburg last summer and he started playing regular senior first-team football for the first time. To say he shone is an understatement: Alajbegovic was named the Austrian Bundesliga’s newcomer of the season, his performances good enough for Bayer to trigger their buy-back clause before the end of March. Simon Rolfes, their managing director for sport, said the player had “not only fulfilled the high expectations placed in him in Salzburg, he has even exceeded them in a very short space of time”. What has surprised people even more than his talent is his complete absence of fear. Despite being only 17, Sergej Barbarez trusted him to take penalties in both World Cup playoff shootouts, while Alajbegovic himself changed the rhythm of both matches after entering from the bench. Inside Bosnia and Herzegovina he is already viewed not only as the country’s biggest prospect, but as one of the future faces of the national team.

data.name: Esmir Bajraktarević
data.description: Years before he became one of Bosnia and Herzegovina’s biggest talents, a home video filmed in Appleton, Wisconsin showed a five-year-old Bajraktarevic playing football in his backyard wearing an outsized Edin Dzeko shirt, celebrating after putting the ball into a small goal. The clip went viral after Bajraktarevic chose Bosnia and Herzegovina over the United States at senior level. “When people ask me where I’m from, I say I’m Bosnian,” said Bajraktarevic, whose parents reached America after fleeing the Srebrenica genocide. “I grew up in a Bosnian family. I speak Bosnian with my parents. It’s my blood, it’s my roots.” There was something poetic about his senior international debut: coming off the bench against the Netherlands in 2024, within five minutes he had set up a goal for Dzeko himself. Fearless in one-on-one situations and seemingly untouched by pressure despite his age, he quickly became one of the faces of Sergej Barbarez’s new generation, and scored the decisive penalty in the shootout victory over Italy that secured Bosnia’s place at the World Cup.

data.name: Stjepan Radeljić
data.description: At 2.01m (6ft 7in) tall, Radeljic is impossible to ignore on a football pitch, but his career has been much less straightforward than his physical presence suggests. Before establishing himself at the highest level, he spent years moving through reserve football, loans and smaller clubs in Croatia, Germany and Moldova, never really being seen as a major prospect. Patience eventually paid off. After helping Sheriff Tiraspol during the most successful period in the club’s history he found real stability at Rijeka, where he developed into one of the leaders of the side that won the Croatian title in 2025. Although naturally associated with aerial duels and physical defending because of his height, Radeljic’s reputation is as an expert in positioning, discipline and consistency.

data.name: Martin Zlomislić
data.description: Zlomislic took a relatively quiet route to the highest level, gradually building his reputation through consistency rather than hype. After developing at Siroki Brijeg, he moved to Rijeka and established himself as one of the more reliable goalkeepers in Croatian football, helping the club negotiate title races and European nights. While Nikola Vasilj remains Bosnia and Herzegovina’s undisputed first-choice, Zlomislic offers the squad an experienced and dependable backup option. He rarely attracts much attention, which for a goalkeeper is usually a good sign. Coaches in Croatia have often praised his composure, positioning and ability to stay calm in difficult moments.

data.name: Haris Tabaković
data.description: Tabakovic’s participation in the World Cup was thrown into doubt in May when he fractured a metatarsal in a league game against Hoffenheim. It was initially assumed that he would have to withdraw, but he insisted he was “doing well” and “will travel to the World Cup”. At 31 it is a chance he may not get again. Born in Switzerland to Bosnian parents, he spent years moving through reserve teams and unsuccessful transfers, scoring goals almost everywhere without ever convincing people he belonged. Then came the breakthrough almost nobody expected: in his late 20s Tabaković suddenly exploded at Hertha Berlin, scoring 22 goals in 32 games and earning a move to Borussia Mönchengladbach and, more important, a Bosnia and Herzegovina call-up. Nearly two years elapsed between his debut and his first international goal but he then got four in five games, including the late equaliser against Italy that, a penalty shootout later, earned Bosnia their World Cup place.

data.name: Arjan Malić
data.description: The young defender was called up as a late replacement for Nidal Celik, just before the tournament started. Offering great versatility, he can play in all positions across the defence. Born in Slovenia, he played for their under-18 and under-19 sides before switching to Bosnia and Herzegovina.  He could also have represented Austria and even had a phone call with Ralf Rangnick and his staff but in the end opted for Bosnia and Herzegovina. "I always had the dream of playing for Bosnia and Herzegovina with Edin Dzeko or Saed Kolasinac. It wasn't a decision against Austria or Slovenia, it was a decision from the heart."

data.name: Jovo Lukić
data.description: Lukic’s path to the Bosnia and Herzegovina World Cup squad has been anything but straightforward. While many attacking players from his generation developed abroad early, he spent years building his career through Bosnian football before moving to Romania in 2024, where his first season at Universitatea Craiova was a failure; only two goals in 31 appearances and growing doubts about whether he belonged at that level. Then everything suddenly changed. Since moving to Universitatea Cluj last summer Lukic’s form has exploded: he finished the campaign as Romania’s top scorer and forced his way into Sergej Barbarez’s plans. “Even if I don’t play, it’s a big thing for me just to be there,” he said. Lukic joked this season that he had not scored a hat-trick “for five years” before finally doing it against Hermannstadt in December, dedicating all three goals to his young son and wheeling out a celebration the boy had asked for.

data.name: Ermin Mahmić
data.description: Only a few days before Sergej Barbarez announced Bosnia and Herzegovina’s World Cup squad, Ermin Mahmic was still officially an Austria youth international. Then came one more successful late-night operation from Barbarez and the sporting director, Emir Spahic, who convinced the Slovan Liberec midfielder to switch allegiances just in time for the tournament. What made the story even more unusual was that Mahmic had already changed direction once before. Born and raised in Austria, he briefly represented Bosnia at under-19 level before returning to Austria’s system, only to switch back again now. “You can’t believe how happy and proud he is to be with us,” Barbarez said after Fifa approved the change. “He has the profile of a player we don’t have much, a player who will continue to develop, with the aim that Bosnia will have good players for the next 10 years.”
